rules.title: Security-relevant events are logged
rules.description: Security-sensitive flows should log meaningful events such as validation failures, authentication outcomes, authorization failures, session failures, application errors, and high-risk actions.
rules.recommendation: Add security event logging at the decision point, with a stable event name and enough context for investigation.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Lists events that should be logged where possible.

rules.semantic_prompt: Look for security-sensitive flows such as authentication, authorization, validation, session handling, application errors, administrative actions, data exports, file uploads, deserialization, or backend connection failures. Flag only if the snippet shows the security decision or failure path and no meaningful logging near that decision.

rules.title: Log entries include investigation context
rules.description: Security log entries need enough context to support monitoring and investigation, including when, where, who, and what details when available.
rules.recommendation: Include stable event names, actor or subject identifiers, action, target object, outcome, severity, reason, and request or correlation identifiers where applicable.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Describes contextual event attributes for log entries.

rules.semantic_prompt: Review logging statements for security-relevant events. Flag logs that are too vague to investigate because they omit available actor, action, target, outcome, reason, severity, request id, or code location context.

rules.title: Sensitive data is excluded from logs
rules.description: Logs should not directly record secrets, credentials, tokens, session identifiers, sensitive personal data, payment data, source code, or database connection strings.
rules.recommendation: Remove, mask, hash, tokenize, or encrypt sensitive values before logging. Prefer stable non-sensitive identifiers for correlation.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Identifies data that should usually not be recorded directly.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Calls out data that may require special treatment before logging.

rules.semantic_prompt: Find logging of secrets, credentials, tokens, session ids, cookies, authorization headers, payment data, PII, source code, connection strings, private keys, internal paths, or internal network identifiers. Flag only when the sensitive value appears to be logged directly without masking or redaction.
deterministic_signals: logging call contains identifier names such as password, token, secret, api_key, authorization, cookie, session_id, ssn, credit_card, connection_string

rules.title: Untrusted event data is sanitized before logging
rules.description: Event data from less trusted zones should be validated, sanitized, and encoded so log entries are not vulnerable to log injection or broken log formatting.
rules.recommendation: Normalize or reject control characters, carriage returns, line feeds, and delimiters before logging untrusted input. Use structured logging fields where possible.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Recommends validation, sanitization, and correct encoding for event data.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Includes testing logs for injection susceptibility.

rules.semantic_prompt: Find logs that include request parameters, headers, payloads, user input, filenames, paths, or external service data. Flag when the data flows into logs without visible validation, sanitization, encoding, structured logging, or safe normalization.
deterministic_signals: logging call contains request, header, param, payload, input, user_agent, or body without nearby sanitize/escape/normalize/replace

rules.title: Application-wide logging mechanism is used
rules.description: Applications should use framework logging or an application-wide logging handler rather than ad hoc console output or scattered custom mechanisms.
rules.recommendation: Route security and exception logging through a standard logger or approved logging module with consistent formatting and field handling.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Recommends suitable framework logging or an application-wide log handler.

rules.semantic_prompt: Flag security or exception logging that relies on print, System.out/System.err, printStackTrace, ad hoc file writes, or custom one-off mechanisms instead of the application's standard logger.

rules.title: Logging failures do not break application flow or leak information
rules.description: Failures in logging processes should not stop the application from otherwise running and should not leak information.
rules.recommendation: Guard custom logging sinks and remote log writers with bounded failure handling, timeouts, and non-sensitive fallback behavior.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Warns that logging failures must not prevent normal application execution or leak information.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Recommends testing behavior when logging infrastructure fails.

rules.semantic_prompt: Look for custom logging code, audit sinks, database log writers, or remote logging calls. Flag when a logging failure can interrupt business logic, expose logging internals, or recurse into more failing logging without a safe fallback.

rules.title: Required logging cannot be completely disabled
rules.description: Logging configuration should keep required application and compliance events available; logging should not be completely disabled by normal configuration.
rules.recommendation: Prevent disabling required security and compliance logs. Treat logging level changes as controlled configuration and verify defaults periodically.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Describes requirements for customizable logging levels.

rules.semantic_prompt: Find configuration or code that can disable application logging, security logging, audit logging, or compliance-required events completely. Flag only when the disabling behavior is evident.

rules.title: Unexpected errors return generic responses and are logged server-side
rules.description: Unexpected errors should produce generic client responses while detailed exception information is logged server-side for investigation.
rules.recommendation: Centralize exception handling. Return a generic response to callers and log exception details through the standard logger on the server side.

source_refs.title: OWASP Error Handling Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Describes generic responses with server-side error logging.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Includes application errors and system events among events to log.

rules.semantic_prompt: Look for exception handlers that return client responses. Flag when unexpected errors are returned without generic messaging, or when details are returned but not logged server-side.
deterministic_signals: except/catch block returns exception message without logger call

rules.title: Technical error details are not exposed to callers
rules.description: Error responses should not reveal stack traces, SQL errors, installation paths, framework internals, or raw exception messages to callers.
rules.recommendation: Return a generic error payload and correlation id. Keep raw exception messages, stack traces, SQL details, and paths in protected server-side logs only.

source_refs.title: OWASP Error Handling Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Shows examples of stack trace and SQL/path disclosure.

source_refs.title: OWASP Error Handling Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Recommends generic responses that do not reveal implementation details.

rules.semantic_prompt: Flag raw exception messages, stack traces, SQL errors, filesystem paths, framework class names, or implementation details being returned to callers, rendered in responses, or included in API error bodies.

rules.title: Caught exceptions are logged or propagated
rules.description: Caught application errors should not be silently swallowed; they should either be handled with meaningful server-side logging or propagated to centralized error handling.
rules.recommendation: Log the exception with stack context or rethrow it to a global handler. Avoid empty catch/except blocks and broad handlers that hide failures.

source_refs.title: OWASP Error Handling Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Detailed exception information should be logged server-side.

source_refs.title: OWASP Logging Cheat Sheet
source_refs.note: Application errors and system events should be logged.

rules.semantic_prompt: Flag catch/except blocks that ignore exceptions, only pass, only return a default value, or only comment TODO without logging or propagation. Do not flag handlers that deliberately recover and log the reason.
deterministic_signals: catch block without log or throw
except block without log or raise
